title: Hydration & Sports Nutrition
summary: Fuel and fluid strategy that actually holds up at practice and on tournament day — plus the myths worth unlearning.

sections.heading: Why hydration and fueling matter
body: An athlete's body is roughly 60% water. Water is the medium for every reaction that keeps muscles firing and the brain thinking clearly, and it is the raw material sweat is made of. Sweating is how the body sheds the heat generated by working muscles (the same thermoregulation system covered in the heat illness unit) — lose too much fluid and that cooling system starts to fail well before an athlete looks or feels 'hot.'

Food is fuel. Carbohydrates, protein, and fat each play a different role in performance and recovery, and getting the basics right is one of the most controllable factors in how an athlete feels in the third quarter or the fourth match of a tournament day — no supplement replaces it.

sections.heading: Dehydration: recognizing it early
body: Thirst is a lagging indicator — by the time an athlete feels thirsty, they are usually already a little behind on fluid. That is why waiting until you're thirsty to drink is not a reliable strategy during practice or competition.

A simple field check is urine color: pale yellow (like lemonade) suggests good hydration; dark yellow or amber suggests the athlete needs more fluid. For athletes training in the heat, weighing in before and after practice is a more precise tool — every pound lost is roughly 16 ounces (2 cups) of sweat that needs to be replaced.
list: Early signs: thirst, dry mouth, dark urine, fatigue, mild headache.
Worsening signs: dizziness, cramping, nausea, decreased performance, irritability.
A body weight loss of more than 2% during exercise (a 150 lb athlete losing 3+ lbs) is linked to measurable drops in performance and rising heat-illness risk.

sections.heading: Practical hydration guidelines
body: There is no single magic number, because sweat rate varies a lot by athlete, sport, and weather — but the pattern is consistent: drink on a schedule before you're thirsty, not in response to thirst.
For hot or long practices, weighing in before and after — and tracking it over the season — lets a team quantify each athlete's personal sweat rate and set a real fluid target instead of guessing.
list: Before: drink fluids in the hours leading up to practice or a game, not just the 10 minutes before.
During: take fluid breaks roughly every 15-20 minutes, more often in heat.
After: rehydrate to replace what was lost — use the pre/post weigh-in difference as a guide, roughly 20-24 oz of fluid per pound lost.

sections.heading: Water vs. sports drinks
body: For most practices and games under about an hour, plain water is enough. Sports drinks (like Gatorade or Powerade) add carbohydrate for energy and electrolytes — mainly sodium — lost in sweat.

Sports drinks earn their place when a session runs longer than about 60 minutes, or any time sweat losses are heavy (hot, humid conditions, salty sweaters who see white residue on their skin or clothes). Outside those situations, water plus normal meals covers it — the extra sugar and calories in sports drinks aren't needed for a 30-minute practice.

callout.title: Energy drinks are not sports drinks
callout.text: Energy drinks (Red Bull, Monster, Bang, etc.) are built around caffeine and stimulants for alertness, not hydration or electrolyte replacement. Caffeine is a mild diuretic and energy drinks are not designed or tested for use during exercise. Don't substitute one for the other — and high school athletes should be cautious with energy drinks generally, especially before or during activity.

sections.heading: Overhydration and hyponatremia — a real but different danger
body: More water is not always better. Drinking far beyond what thirst and sweat losses call for — especially plain water over several hours during a long event like a cross-country meet, marathon, or all-day tournament — can dilute the sodium in the blood. This condition, called hyponatremia, can cause confusion, nausea, swelling, seizures, and in rare severe cases can be fatal.

The practical takeaway for students: hydration guidance is about matching fluid to losses, not maximizing fluid intake. 'Drink when thirsty, and use body weight changes as a check' protects against both dehydration and overhydration.

image.description: Two-panel side-by-side comparison. Left panel labeled 'Dehydration': thirst, dry mouth, dark urine, fatigue icons. Right panel labeled 'Overhydration / Hyponatremia': confusion, worsening headache, vomiting, swollen hands/feet icons. Add a small caption under the right panel: 'More water makes this WORSE, not better.' Clean flat infographic style.
image.alt: Comparison diagram of dehydration signs versus overhydration (hyponatremia) signs during long endurance events.

callout.title: Red flag — refer out
callout.text: An athlete who has been drinking large amounts of plain water over a long event and develops confusion, worsening headache, vomiting, or swollen hands/feet may have exertional hyponatremia, not dehydration — treating it like dehydration by giving more water can make it worse. This is a medical emergency: stop activity and get emergency medical care immediately. Never assume 'more fluid' is the fix for a distance athlete who looks unwell late in a long event.

sections.heading: Fueling for training and games
body: Carbohydrates are the body's primary fuel for moderate-to-high intensity exercise — they're stored in muscles and the liver as glycogen and burned quickly during a game or hard practice. Foods like whole grains, fruit, rice, and pasta should make up the largest share of an athlete's plate, especially around training days.

Protein repairs and rebuilds the muscle tissue broken down during training. High school athletes get what they need from food — chicken, fish, eggs, dairy, beans, nuts — spread across meals and snacks throughout the day, rather than concentrated in one big serving.
Skipping breakfast on game day is a common mistake: it leaves an athlete running on empty glycogen stores by the second half. Even a light meal is better than nothing.

sections.heading: Timing: before and after activity
body: What and when an athlete eats around a workout affects how they feel during it and how well they recover after it.
list: 3-4 hours before: a larger, balanced meal with carbs, moderate protein, and lower fat/fiber (which digest slowly and can cause GI discomfort during exercise) — for example chicken, rice, and vegetables.
1-2 hours before: a lighter carb-focused snack if a full meal isn't possible — a banana, granola bar, or toast with jam.
Within about 30-60 minutes after a hard session: a combined carb + protein snack to jump-start recovery — chocolate milk, a turkey sandwich, or yogurt with fruit — followed by a normal meal.

image.description: Horizontal timeline graphic centered on a practice/game icon, with markers at '3-4 hours before' (balanced meal icon: chicken, rice, vegetables), '1-2 hours before' (light snack icon: banana or granola bar), and '30-60 minutes after' (recovery snack icon: chocolate milk or sandwich), followed by 'normal meal.' Clean flat infographic timeline style.
image.alt: Timeline diagram of pre-activity and post-activity meal and snack timing for athletes.

sections.heading: Myths worth unlearning
body: A few beliefs are common in locker rooms and on social media but do not hold up, and coaches and athletes should know the safer, evidence-based alternative.
list: Myth: 'You need protein powder or shakes to build muscle.' Reality — high school athletes can meet protein needs through food; whole foods also supply the carbs, vitamins, and minerals a shake doesn't.

Myth: 'Sweating off water weight before a weigh-in is a safe way to make a class.' Reality — deliberate dehydration (sitting in a sauna, restricting fluids, exercising in plastic suits) to 'cut weight' impairs performance, strains the heart and kidneys, and has caused wrestler deaths — it is dangerous, not a shortcut.

Myth: 'An energy drink is basically a sports drink with extra kick.' Reality — see the caffeine warning above; they serve different purposes and energy drinks are not appropriate hydration during exercise.
Myth: 'If a little sports drink helps, a lot must help more.' Reality — matching intake to actual losses matters; overdoing plain fluid intake risks hyponatremia (see above).

sections.heading: What to actually pack
body: Good fueling doesn't require anything fancy — it requires planning ahead so the right things are in the gym bag or cooler instead of relying on vending machines between games at a tournament.
list: Gym bag basics: a refillable water bottle, a banana or other easy fruit, a granola bar or pretzels, and a change of dry socks/shirt (wet gear makes it harder to regulate temperature next round).

Between games at a tournament: something carb-based and easy to digest — pretzels, crackers, a bagel, fruit, or a sports drink if the next game is soon and sweat losses have been heavy. Avoid a large, fatty meal (like fast food) right before playing again.
End of a long tournament day: a real meal with carbs and protein once competition is done, plus continued fluid replacement — don't wait until the ride home to start rehydrating.

quiz.question: Why is thirst an unreliable signal for when to start hydrating during exercise?
choices: Thirst only occurs after an athlete is already overhydrated
Thirst is caused by hunger, not fluid loss
Thirst typically lags behind the body's actual fluid needs
Thirst does not change during exercise
quiz.explanation: By the time an athlete feels thirsty, they are usually already somewhat behind on fluid replacement, which is why hydration plans use scheduled drinking rather than waiting for thirst.

quiz.question: A wrestler weighs 148 lbs before practice and 144 lbs after, with no bathroom breaks in between. About how much fluid should be replaced?
choices: Exactly 4 oz, since only 4 lbs were lost
Roughly 80-96 oz (based on 20-24 oz per pound lost)
None — weight loss during practice is muscle, not fluid
A single 8 oz cup of water is always sufficient regardless of weight lost
quiz.explanation: Each pound of body weight lost during exercise is mostly sweat, and roughly 20-24 oz of fluid per pound lost is the general replacement guideline — 4 lbs lost means about 80-96 oz to replace.

quiz.question: For a 30-minute indoor practice in mild conditions, what is the most appropriate fluid choice?
choices: A sports drink, because any exercise requires electrolyte replacement
An energy drink for the caffeine boost
No fluid is needed for sessions under an hour
Plain water
quiz.explanation: Water is enough for shorter, lower-sweat sessions. Sports drinks earn their place in sessions longer than about 60 minutes or with heavy sweat losses; energy drinks are not a hydration tool at all.

quiz.question: What is the main difference between a sports drink and an energy drink?
choices: Sports drinks replace carbohydrate and electrolytes lost in sweat; energy drinks are built around caffeine/stimulants and are not designed for hydration during exercise
They are functionally interchangeable for use during a game
Energy drinks contain more water than sports drinks
Sports drinks contain caffeine and energy drinks do not
quiz.explanation: Sports drinks are formulated to replace sweat losses (fluid, sodium, carbohydrate). Energy drinks are stimulant-focused for alertness and are not appropriate substitutes during exercise — caffeine does not equal hydration.

quiz.question: A cross-country runner has been drinking large amounts of plain water throughout a 3-hour race and now seems confused with a worsening headache and swollen hands. What is the most likely explanation, and what should happen?
choices: Simple dehydration — give more water right away
Normal fatigue — let them rest and keep drinking water
Possible exertional hyponatremia (diluted blood sodium) — treat as a medical emergency and get emergency care immediately
A muscle cramp — stretch the affected area and continue racing
quiz.explanation: Excess plain water intake over a long event can dilute blood sodium (hyponatremia), causing confusion and swelling. Giving more water worsens it — this is a medical emergency, not simple dehydration.

quiz.question: Which macronutrient is the body's primary fuel source for moderate-to-high intensity exercise?
choices: Protein, broken down for immediate energy
Carbohydrate, stored as glycogen in muscle and liver
Fat, which is used first in every sport
Fiber, which digests quickly during exercise
quiz.explanation: Carbohydrate is stored as glycogen and is the primary fuel burned during moderate-to-high intensity effort. Protein's main role is tissue repair, not immediate fuel, and fat/fiber digest too slowly to be primary in-game fuel sources.

quiz.question: An athlete has a game at 6 pm. What is the best pre-game eating plan?
choices: Nothing after lunch, to avoid feeling full during the game
A large, high-fat fast food meal 30 minutes before kickoff
Skip food entirely and rely on a sports drink for all game-day energy
A larger balanced meal around 2-3 pm, with a light carb snack around 4:30-5 pm if needed
quiz.explanation: A larger meal 3-4 hours before activity, followed by a light carb snack 1-2 hours before if needed, tops off glycogen without causing GI discomfort. Skipping food or eating a large fatty meal right before play both hurt performance.

quiz.question: A wrestler wants to 'cut weight' before a weigh-in by sitting in a sauna and restricting fluids the day before. What is the correct guidance?
choices: This is dangerous rapid dehydration that impairs performance and stresses the heart and kidneys — it should not be done
This is safe as long as it's done only once per season
This is recommended because 'water weight' doesn't affect performance
This is fine as long as the athlete drinks a sports drink immediately afterward
quiz.explanation: Deliberate rapid dehydration to make weight has caused serious injury and death in wrestlers. It is never a safe practice, regardless of how it's timed or what is consumed afterward.

flashcards.front: Why thirst is a poor hydration cue
flashcards.back: Thirst lags behind the body's actual fluid needs — by the time you feel thirsty, you're already somewhat behind on fluid.
Pale yellow (like lemonade) = well hydrated. Dark yellow/amber = needs more fluid.
Losing more than 2% of body weight in sweat during exercise (e.g., 3+ lbs for a 150 lb athlete) is linked to reduced performance and rising heat-illness risk.
Roughly 20-24 oz of fluid per pound of body weight lost during exercise.

flashcards.front: When do sports drinks 'earn their place'?
flashcards.back: Sessions longer than about 60 minutes, or any session with heavy sweat losses (hot/humid conditions, salty sweaters). Otherwise, water is enough.
Energy drinks are built around caffeine/stimulants for alertness, not hydration — they are not a substitute for sports drinks during exercise.
Dangerously low blood sodium from drinking far more fluid (especially plain water) than the body is losing, usually during long endurance events. Causes confusion, swelling, and can be fatal.
They can look similar, but giving more water to someone with hyponatremia makes it worse. Confusion/swelling late in a long event is a medical emergency.
Primary fuel for moderate-to-high intensity exercise, stored as glycogen in muscle and liver.
Repairs and rebuilds muscle tissue; best obtained from food, spread across meals throughout the day rather than one large serving.
Larger balanced meal 3-4 hours before activity; light carb snack 1-2 hours before if needed.
A carb + protein snack within about 30-60 minutes after a hard session (e.g., chocolate milk, turkey sandwich) jump-starts recovery.
